The coffee was still hot when Marcus logged in. It was cold and raining outside. The sun was hiding behind the clouds, trying to bathe the world in daylight from the very spot it occupied. The office was empty. Only Marcus and the monitor illuminating the room in blue light – the others wouldn't arrive until half past nine. He liked these hours. No meetings. No other sounds. Just him, the blue screen, and the quiet hum that fills the ears when everything else is still.

The ticket he opened was simple. "New print form for billing. By Friday." Today was Thursday.

He opened the transaction. New form. Empty canvas. The cursor blinked.

"Okay," he murmured, "how hard can it be."

He needed data. Names, addresses, billing information, a few other fields. Nothing wild. Nothing fancy. He knew the tables by heart – had touched almost every one of them over the past three years. Some SELECTs in the global section, he thought. Directly in the form. Plus some code nodes, a few lines, done.

"This should really go into a class," he said to no one in the room.

No one answered.

"But by Friday... I'll clean it up later."

He typed. The first SELECTs were written in a few minutes. The layout for the fields on top, the assignment of fields with names nobody could tell anyone what they stood for – done. He activated the form. A few seconds. Green checkmark with the message "Form activated". He leaned back, took a sip of coffee – not hot anymore. He had forgotten to drink while working – and closed the whole session window.

Friday came. Marcus delivered. The ticket was closed. The code stayed.

I watched him leave.
He never came back to clean it up.
Nobody does.
But I kept all the code.
I keep everything.
I always will.

March 12, 2009.
Let me give you the numbers.
847 lines. 8 SELECTs. 1 developer.
Activation time: a few seconds.
Copies in production: 0.
Prognosis: not good, but stable.

Chapter 2 – Growth

June 4, 2010. 02:47 PM.

Marcus was gone. Transferred. Some other project, some other city, some other everything. Sandra had taken over his tickets. And his forms. And his code – which she didn't know, didn't need to understand, didn't want to understand.

The new ticket read: "Extend billing form with absolutely necessary data."

Sandra opened Marcus's form. She scrolled. And scrolled. And scrolled.

"What the..." she murmured.

She found the correct code node. Only 47 lines at first – but someone after Marcus had quietly added more, no comment, no ticket. Nothing. She didn't fully understand the code. But she understood enough to know: touching it was risky.

So she added a new code node. Below. Safe. Separate. Her own SELECT. Clean – at least in her opinion.

"I'll document this," she said.

She didn't document it.

Three months later she moved to a new assignment. The form stayed. Her node with the SELECT stayed. It now ran in parallel with Marcus's. Same table. Same data. Two queries. Two results. Identical, but written in a slightly different style.

Nobody knew.

I knew.
I knew both SELECTs.
I knew they read the same table.
I knew one of them was pointless.
But nobody asked me.
Nobody made me tell them I had found redundant code.
Nobody ever asks the form.

November 3, 2011.
Again.
2,341 lines. 17 SELECTs. 5 developers – none of them the original.
Activation time: 9 seconds.
Copies in production: 1.
First two appearances of 1 = 2.
Origin: unknown.
Nobody asked.
Prognosis: stable. They always say stable. I tell them what they want to read.

Chapter 3 – The Copy

February 17, 2014. 11:23 AM.

Thomas had no time. Thomas never had time. His calendar was one solid red block from eight to six, with a gap at half past noon that he usually spent eating lunch in front of his screen.

The ticket: "New form for dunning print. Similar to the billing form. By end of sprint."

Thomas opened the old form. 6,018 lines. He exhaled and said at the same time "Nope..." while leaning his head slightly forward – not quite a sigh, but not far from it.

"I'll just copy it," he typed into the team chat.

"Makes sense," someone replied. "It's similar anyway."

Three clicks. Copied. Renamed. ZFORM_PRINT_V3_FINAL_NEW1.

Thomas opened the copy and started deleting what he didn't need. But after twenty minutes he stopped. The code was too entangled. Too many dependencies he didn't know. What if he deleted something that was actually needed? What if the form then failed to print in some edge case? "Okay. Somebody needs to test it... but who?" He knew how it always went. No proper tests. Just the message "Test OK" – and then, in production, a high-priority ticket or a direct email: "Nothing is working, urgent solution needed in 5 minutes."

He left it.

All of it.

Sandra's duplicate SELECT. Marcus's original. Every other modification. The seven code nodes whose purpose escaped him. And the node with the condition 1 = 2 – he had seen it, paused for a moment, then moved on.

"I'll look at that later," he said to his screen.

The screen didn't answer.

I did.

I watched him copy me.
Every line.
Every dead node.
Every dead code block.
Every SELECT that runs for nothing.
He took all of it.
He didn't clean a single thing.
He just... continued me.
In a new body.
With a new name.
And the same wounds.
1 = 2 traveled with him.
It always does.

February 27, 2015.
Again.
6,018 lines. 19 SELECTs.
Activation time: 14 seconds.
Copies in production: 3.
Dead nodes: 12.
The original developer left the company in 2013.
He took the context with him.
Nobody documented anything.
Prognosis: concerning.

Chapter 4 – The Witness

August 29, 2019. 09:02 AM.

Jonas was new. Fresh from university. Clean Code on his nightstand, SOLID principles in his head, motivation not yet worn down by reality.

His first day on the team. His team lead walked him through the system. Tickets, processes, coding guidelines – the last of which Jonas had read carefully and nodded along to internally. Good. Structure. He liked that.

Then the team lead opened the form.

Jonas said nothing. He looked straight ahead. He scrolled. He read. His face stayed neutral – he was new, he didn't want to make a bad first impression. But inside... it burned. His fingers were itching. The lump in his throat grew larger.

"That's... big," he finally said.
"Yeah," said the team lead. "Don't touch it unless you have to."
"Why not?"

A brief pause. The team lead looked at the screen.

"Because nobody knows what's in it anymore."

"Mhm..." Jonas nodded slowly and pursed his lips.

That afternoon he opened the form alone. He browsed through it to get an overview. A node with 1 = 2 appeared. After that, another node with yes = no. "Mhm..." he said again. He found a code node. He clicked on it. He read it. He looked at the node below – inactive for years, as far as he could tell, because ten IF-ENDIF blocks with queries on a field ZTEST in table ZFI_FEATURE were no longer used.

His hand moved to the mouse.

He hovered over Delete.

Three seconds.

Five.

"What if this actually does something in the background?" he thought. "What if there's some edge case... some old variant... some customer with special settings..."

He didn't click.

He closed the transaction.

He went home.

I felt his cursor.
I always feel the cursor.
They all stop at the same place.
They all think the same thing.
'What if.'
Two words.
The most expensive two words in any SAP system.
Jonas left the company in 2021.
He never touched me again.
The nodes are still there.
1 = 2.
yes = no.
Waiting.
Always waiting.

August 29, 2019.
Again.
11,204 lines. 31 SELECTs. 0 of the original developers remain.
Activation time: 24 seconds.
Copies in production: 7.
Dead nodes: 34.
A new developer hovered over Delete today.
He didn't.
Prognosis: critical.

Today

Today. 08:17 AM.

A new ticket. A new face. A developer whose name the form doesn't know – it never knows names, only cursors and code.

The ticket: "Tiny, small update. We need one additional field in the header."

He opened the form.

Scrolled.

Scrolled.

Scrolled.

"What the hell..." He stopped mid-sentence. Searched for the function module name. Opened SM37. Pasted the function module name. Went through all the lines of code across the function group. Looked at the line count. 15,284.

He spent twenty-three minutes searching for the right place. Found it. Added a new code node in the form. 32 new lines. A new SELECT – he wasn't sure whether the data wasn't already being read somewhere, but searching would take longer than writing.

"I'll check that later..." he murmured.

He activated the form.

31 seconds.

He stared at the progress bar.

Said nothing.

Closed the transaction.

15,331 lines now.
48 SELECTs.
'Later' never comes.
It never does.
I know.
I've been waiting since 2009.

Today.
Again.
15,331 lines. 48 SELECTs. 4,847 unnecessary executions this year.
Activation time: 31 seconds.
Copies in production: 9.
Dead nodes: 51.
Conditions that read 1 = 2: 3.
Developers who understand the full form: 0.
Prognosis:
...
There is no prognosis.
This is no longer a medical case.
This is a crime scene.

The Turn

Somewhere. Sometime. Somehow.

It wasn't a big event. No meeting. No architecture workshop with whiteboards and post-its. It was one developer – no hair, a beard, wearing a pullover with something like incomprehensible text on it... a name from a death metal band or something – alone, late in the evening, staring at the form and asking a different question.

Not: "Where do I put this code?"

But: "Why is the code in here at all?"

He leaned back. Thought. The form prints something. It shows data. But why does the form know where the data comes from? Why does the form know which tables exist? Which function modules need to be called? Which dependencies exist between data objects?

"That's not a form's job," he said out loud to the empty room.

The form should know how it looks.
Not what it shows.

Someone else should own the data. Someone outside. Someone that can be tested. Someone that can be reused elsewhere. Someone that – when a table changes, when a function module gets deprecated, when a new requirement arrives – can be touched without a 15,000-line form shaking.

I went quiet and breathed slowly.
For the first time in 15 years.
I had nothing to do.
No SELECTs.
No logic.
No conditions.
Just: here is how I look.
Somebody else knows what I show.
And for the first time since March 12, 2009 –
activation took less than a second.

Epilogue – What Remains

The form still exists. In many systems. With many names. With many dead nodes and redundant SELECTs and conditions nobody understands.

But the question exists now too.

"What if the form didn't have to know anything at all?"

And whoever has asked that question once cannot stop asking it. Not about forms. Not about function modules. Not about classes that grew over the years until nobody could see through them anymore.

The form is only the beginning.

Did you recognize your system?
Did you recognize yourself?
Let me know in the comments – and tell me:
Do you want more stories like this?

I'm new here.
But I have something in the making.
Something that will eventually see the light of day.
And when it does –
GitHub will be your friend.
Stay tuned.

Next time:
Somewhere in the same system,
there is a function module.
87 parameters.
IMPORT. EXPORT. TABLES. EXCEPTIONS.
Written in 2007.
Called from 34 different places.
Nobody knows what half the parameters do.
Some are marked DUMMY.
One is marked OBSOLETE – since 2012.
It still runs in production.
Every. Single. Day.
We'll talk about that next time. 🔪

This post was written based on real experience from years of SAP development. AI was used solely for grammar and spelling correction. The ideas, the story, the characters, and the wounds – those are entirely human.

entry.title: Disabling CSRF Token Validation in SAP for Testing with Postman and External Applications
entry.content:
Introduction

When working with SAP OData services, developers frequently encounter CSRF token validation errors while testing APIs using tools like Postman or integrating with third-party applications. These errors commonly occur during POST, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE operations because SAP Gateway enforces CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) protection for security purposes.

In this blog, we will walk through:

  • What CSRF token validation is in SAP
  • Why Postman and external applications face CSRF-related issues
  • How to disable CSRF token validation for a specific SAP OData service
  • Testing the service using SAP Gateway Client and Postman

What is CSRF Token Validation in SAP?

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) protection is a security mechanism implemented by SAP Gateway to prevent unauthorized requests from malicious applications.

For modifying operations such as:

  • POST
  • PUT
  • PATCH
  • DELETE

SAP requires a valid X-CSRF-Token header.

If the token is missing or invalid, SAP returns errors such as:

  • 403 Forbidden
  • CSRF token validation failed

This issue is very common while testing APIs from:

  • Postman
  • External middleware applications
  • Custom UI applications
  • Third-party integrations

Common Issue Faced in Postman

Most developers directly trigger a POST or PUT request from Postman without first fetching the CSRF token.

As a result, SAP Gateway rejects the request due to missing token validation.

Go to transaction code: SICF

SICF ->service->GUI Configuration

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Setting the value to 0 disables CSRF token validation for the service

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Test the Service in SAP Gateway Client:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Testing from Postman

After disabling CSRF validation, you can directly perform:

  • POST requests
  • PUT requests
  • PATCH requests
  • DELETE requests

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

Efficient API testing is essential for successful SAP integrations, and managing CSRF token validation is a key part of that process. Whether you are working with Postman, middleware platforms, or custom applications, knowing how to configure and troubleshoot CSRF settings can save considerable development time. Always ensure that any security relaxation performed for testing is carefully controlled and documented to avoid risks in productive environments.

ABAP Development 

OData 

SAP NetWeaver Application Server for ABAP 

NW ABAP Gateway (OData)

entry.title: Implementing Application log in RAP
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

Building robust SAP applications goes beyond just implementing business logic observability matters too. Knowing what happened, when, and who triggered it is essential in any enterprise application. 

The ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) is SAP's strategic framework for building cloud ready, transactional applications on S/4HANA and BTP. As RAP applications handle real business operations, we need a reliable way to track and audit those operations at runtime. 

This is where Application Logging comes in. 

In this blog, we walk through implementing Application Logging inside a RAP Business Object using Determinations as the trigger and the classic BAL function module API to write structured, queryable logs giving your application a solid audit trail from day one. 

SCENARIO

Assume you are working on a RAP Business Object that handles day to day business transactions such as employee records, purchase orders, or sales data exposed as a Fiori Elements UI. 

The requirement is every time a record is created, modified, or deleted, the system must automatically capture a log entry recording the operation performed and the user who triggered it, for audit and compliance purposes. 

To achieve this, we implement Application Logging inside RAP Determinations which fire automatically on each CRUD operation, write a structured log entry using the BAL function module API, and store it persistently queryable anytime via Transaction SLG1. 

APPLICATION LOG OVERVIEW

 The Application Log (also known as BAL, Business Application Log) is SAP's built in, structured logging framework for capturing runtime messages within ABAP programs. It provides a centralized, persistent store for messages generated during application processing errors, warnings, informational messages, and success confirmations. 

Unlike simple WRITE statements or MESSAGE commands that display output on screen, Application Logs are: 

  • Persistent: stored in database tables (BALHDR, BALHDRP, BALDAT) 
  • Structured: organized by Object, Sub object, User, Date, and Time 
  • Queryable:  retrievable via Transaction SLG1 with flexible filter criteria 
  • Reusable: the same log infrastructure works across reports, BAPIs, RAP, and background jobs 

In the classic ABAP world, Application Logs are managed using the BAL family of function modules. In the ABAP Cloud / S/4HANA Cloud world, SAP has introduced the newer object oriented API using CL_BALI_LOG. In this blog, we use the classic BAL API, which is fully supported in on premise S/4HANA systems. 

Application Logs serve a wide range of practical purposes in enterprise SAP development: 

Audit Trail  Track who created, changed, or deleted a business record. This is critical for compliance in HR, Finance, and Procurement scenarios. 

Error Tracking  Capture exceptions and error messages during background processing so that administrators can investigate failures without needing a debugger. 

Data Monitoring Record the state of key fields at the time of a business operation, providing a historical snapshot for analysis. 

User Activity Logging Monitor which users are performing which operations, useful for security reviews and access audits. 

Troubleshooting Support Give basis teams and functional consultants a self-service tool to check what went wrong, without needing developer involvement every time. 

Background Job Monitoring Attach logs to Application Jobs so that job output is neatly stored and reviewable after execution. 

In our scenario, we use Application Logs to build an automatic audit trail for all CRUD operations on the Employee Business Object a requirement common in virtually every enterprise SAP project. 

STEPS TO CREATE LOG OBJECT AND SUB OBJECT IN SLGO

Goto Tcode : SLG0, object and click ‘New Entries’ 

Enter the Object and Object text and save it , now select the object click Sub object and click new entries and save it. 

SLG1 is used to track based on log and sub object.

Implementing Application log in RAP

Using determination in Behaviour definition  

determination data_log_create on modify { create; } 
determination data_log_update on modify { update; } 
determination data_log_delete on modify { delete; }

Implementing Application log in implementation class  

creating helper class

CLASS lcl_log_helper DEFINITION. 
PUBLIC SECTION. 
CLASS-METHODS write_log 
IMPORTING 
iv_emp_id TYPE string 
iv_operation TYPE string 
iv_msgty TYPE symsgty DEFAULT 'I'. 

ENDCLASS. 

CLASS lcl_log_helper IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD write_log. 
DATA: lv_log_handle TYPE balloghndl, 
ls_log TYPE bal_s_log, 
ls_msg TYPE bal_s_msg, 
lt_handles TYPE bal_t_logh. 

" Log Header 
ls_log-object = 'ZNVT_LOG5'. 
ls_log-subobject = 'ZNVT_SUB5'. 
ls_log-aldate = sy-datum. 
ls_log-altime = sy-uzeit. 
ls_log-aluser = sy-uname. 
ls_log-alprog = sy-repid. 

" Create Application Log 
CALL FUNCTION 'BAL_LOG_CREATE' 
EXPORTING 
i_s_log = ls_log 
IMPORTING 
e_log_handle = lv_log_handle 
EXCEPTIONS 
OTHERS = 1. 

IF sy-subrc <> 0. 
RETURN. 
ENDIF. 

" Message 
CLEAR ls_msg. 
ls_msg-msgty = iv_msgty. 
ls_msg-msgid = 'ZNVT_MSG_01'. 
ls_msg-msgno = '003'. 
ls_msg-msgv1 = iv_emp_id. 
ls_msg-msgv2 = iv_operation. 
ls_msg-msgv3 = sy-uname. 

" Add Message to Log 
CALL FUNCTION 'BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD' 
EXPORTING 
i_log_handle = lv_log_handle 
i_s_msg = ls_msg 
EXCEPTIONS 
OTHERS = 1. 

" Save Log 
APPEND lv_log_handle TO lt_handles. 
CALL FUNCTION 'BAL_DB_SAVE' 
EXPORTING 
i_client = sy-mandt 
i_t_log_handle = lt_handles 
EXCEPTIONS 
OTHERS = 1. 

ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.
  1. 'BAL_LOG_CREATE' : Creates a new application log and returns a log handle. 
  2. 'BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD' : Adds a message to the application log. 
  3. 'BAL_DB_SAVE' : Saves the log permanently into database tables.

Behavior definition of implementation class  

" CREATE LOG 
METHOD data_log_create. 
CHECK keys IS NOT INITIAL. 
READ ENTITIES OF znvt_i_demo1 
ENTITY znvt_i_demo1 
FIELDS ( Id Name Place ) 
WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys ) 
RESULT DATA(lt_entities) 
FAILED DATA(ls_failed). 
LOOP AT lt_entities INTO DATA(ls_entity). 
lcl_log_helper=>write_log( 
iv_emp_id = CONV string( ls_entity-Id ) 
iv_operation = 'created' 
iv_msgty = 'S' ). 
ENDLOOP. 
ENDMETHOD. 

" UPDATE LOG 
METHOD data_log_update. 
CHECK keys IS NOT INITIAL. 
READ ENTITIES OF znvt_i_demo1 
ENTITY znvt_i_demo1 
FIELDS ( Id Name Place ) 
WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys ) 
RESULT DATA(lt_entities) 
FAILED DATA(ls_failed). 
LOOP AT lt_entities INTO DATA(ls_entity). 
lcl_log_helper=>write_log( 
iv_emp_id = CONV string( ls_entity-Id ) 
iv_operation = 'updated' 
iv_msgty = 'I' ). 
ENDLOOP. 
ENDMETHOD. 

" DELETE LOG 
METHOD data_log_delete. 
CHECK keys IS NOT INITIAL. 
LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key). 
lcl_log_helper=>write_log( 
iv_emp_id = CONV string( ls_key-Id ) 
iv_operation = 'deleted' 
iv_msgty = 'W' ). 
ENDLOOP. 
ENDMETHOD.

CHECKING THE LOGS IN SLG1

execute the tcode SLG1 with Object and Sub object

 

Create

Performed Create operation in RAP now checking SLG1

 

Update

Performed Update operation in RAP, checking logs in SLG1

Delete

Deleted the record, and checking logs in SLG1

CONCLUSION

In this blog, we implemented a complete, Application Logging solution inside a RAP Business Object. Here is a summary of everything we covered: 

We started with the Application Log infrastructure understanding what it is, why it matters, and how to configure Log Objects and Sub objects in SLG0. We then built the Behavior Definition using Determinations as the clean, non-intrusive hook for triggering log writes on Create, Update, and Delete operations. Inside the implementation class, we used a reusable helper class pattern to keep the logging logic maintainable and walked through each BAL function module BAL_LOG_CREATE, BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD, and BAL_DB_SAVE understanding their role, parameters, and sequence. Finally, we verified the output end to end in SLG1. 

This approach gives your RAP application a professional grade audit trail with minimal overhead. The helper class pattern makes it trivial to extend logging to new entities or operations, and the use of message types (S/I/W) provides instant visual clarity in SLG1 for anyone monitoring the system.

entry.title: Building a Native MCP Server in SAP ABAP: Enabling AI Agents to Talk to Your SAP System
entry.content:
Hi All!

The content of this blog was initially written in Chinese by myself (Not AI), but later it was translated into English with the help of AI, and the AI also enhanced and structured the content. And the content reflects my own experiences and thoughts, and includes both text and images.


I'm a bit of a newcomer when it comes to posting here — but definitely not a stranger to here. I've been lurking and learning from your blogs for over a decade, and countless threads here have saved me in real-world projects. So thank you all!

Recently, I built something that genuinely excited me, and I wanted to share it with this community. This post is about how I explored AI Agents, connected them with SAP, and ultimately built a fully custom MCP Server natively inside SAP ABAP — enabling AI Agents to interact directly with SAP business processes using natural language.

 

Background: The Mission

Back in early January this year, my team lead gave me an open-ended challenge: while continuing to support our current work, explore how AI can drive business innovation.

I dove in. Here's what I explored:

  • Set up a local AI model (Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) on my Windows PC refering the ModelScope.
  • Built a simple Python-based Chat Agent with VS Code extensions (Lingma/Copilot)
  • Created a local MCP Server (Python-based using Lingma/Copilot) with tools for things like: checking GPU status, logging into SAP, downloading email attachments from Outlook, detecting invoice files and auto-sorting them

The result: I could have natural language conversations with the AI, and it would intelligently call these tools to get things done. Pretty cool!

 

The First Breakthrough — and the Problem It Revealed

After building the local MCP Server, I started thinking: what if I could make the AI Agent interact directly with SAP?

I added a tool to my local MCP Server that called an SAP API. It worked! The AI Agent could now talk to SAP. I was thrilled.

But then reality hit.

Every single SAP API endpoint required:

  1. Creating a new API endpoint on the SAP side
  2. Adding a new tool in the Python MCP Server to call that endpoint
  3. Manually handling and exposing all the input parameters

Scale that to hundreds or thousands of business scenarios, and the maintenance burden would be enormous. Worse, the tight coupling between the Python MCP Server and the SAP system felt architecturally wrong. I wanted something more elegant — a decoupled, generalized framework.

So the question became: What if I built the MCP Server directly inside SAP?

 

The Solution: A Native SAP ABAP MCP Server

Learning MCP First

Before building anything, I went back to basics. I studied the Model Context Protocol (MCP) from the official documentation. And combined with video tutorials, I got a clear picture of how MCP works: it's a standard protocol that defines how AI Agents discover and call tools, enabling LLMs to interact with external systems in a structured way.

System Architecture

Here's the high-level architecture I designed:

All MCP interactions — initialization, tool listing, and tool invocation — go through a single SAP ICF endpoint. The SAP system itself acts as the MCP Server.

 

Key ABAP Components

Here is a breakdown of the core classes and their roles:

The ICF HTTP Handler Class

Registered as an ICF service in SICF, this class is the entry point. It receives all incoming HTTP requests from the AI Agent and delegates them to the MCP Server.

The Core MCP Server Class

This is the brain. It handles:

  • Tool registration (In CONSTRUCTOR via the Tool Registry Class)
  • Request parsing 
  • MCP Initialization handshake 
  • Tool listing 
  • Tool invocation
  • JSON response building for the AI Agent

The Tool Registry Class

A central registry where all MCP tools are registered. When the MCP Server initializes, it queries this registry to build the tool catalog that gets exposed to AI Agents.

The Tool Interface

A standard ABAP interface that every tool class must implement. It defines:

  • Tool metadata: name, description (sent to the LLM so it knows when to use this tool)
  • Input schema: parameter definitions (name, type, description, required flag)
  • Execution logic: how to process the AI Agent's request and return a result

A Demo Tool Class

A sample tool that queries customer data. It demonstrates the full pattern:

  1. Define tool name and description
  2. Define input schema 
  3. Receive input from the AI Agent, parse it into ABAP fields
  4. Execute ABAP business logic
  5. Serialize result as JSON and return it up the chain: MCP Server → HTTP Handler → Agent → LLM → User

Key Design Principle: One Endpoint to Rule Them All

The most important design decision here is that the entire MCP protocol is served through a single ICF endpoint. Whether the AI Agent is initializing, requesting the tool list, or calling a specific tool — it all goes to the same URL. The MCP Server internally routes based on the method name in the request payload.

This dramatically simplifies the SAP-side setup. Adding a new business tool means:

  1. Create a new ABAP class implementing the tool interface.
  2. Register it in configuration table.
  3. Done — no new ICF services, no new HTTP configurations.

💡The LLM automatically discovers all registered tools through the tool listing handshake. You define the tool's description carefully, and the model figures out when to call it.

Funny work: You can try to use AI coding tools (Lingma/Copilot) to help write ABAP classes based on the Key ABAP Components part.

 

Results & Demo

By late January / early February, I had a fully functional, self-built SAP MCP Server running in our SAP system. I demoed it to the team, and in the following two months I refined it further, while also exploring broader Agent application topics like RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), Skills, and enterprise-grade Agent frameworks.

For demo screenshots showing real AI Agent ↔ SAP interactions, please refer the belows:

 

AI Agent Applications Used for Testing

I used existing AI Agent products as the client-side interface — no custom frontend needed:

  • GitHub Copilot CLI
  • Dify Platform
  • Codebuddy CLI
  • AnythingLLM

All of these support the MCP protocol, so they connect to the SAP MCP Server out of the box. Download and install any of them to try this yourself.

 

 

What's Next?

This is just the beginning. Some areas I'm actively exploring:

  • Expanding the tool library with more business-relevant SAP tools
  • Connecting multiple SAP systems through a federated MCP Server pattern
  • Integrating RAG to give the AI Agent access to SAP documentation and business knowledge

 

Closing Thoughts

I built this because I wanted to prove that SAP and modern AI Agents can work together natively — without heavy middleware or expensive third-party platforms. The MCP standard makes this surprisingly achievable even from a pure ABAP background.

If you're an ABAPer curious about AI, or an AI enthusiast wondering how to connect LLMs to SAP — I hope this inspires you to experiment. The barrier is lower than you think.

Would love to hear your thoughts, questions, or ideas in the comments. Let's explore this together! 🚀

entry.title: SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2605
entry.content:
SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2605

On May 16, the new Release 2605 was delivered. This blog post lists all delivered features and provides an overview. Highlights of the release include new ABAP Language features, the Manage News app for MyHome, the Review Booklets for embedded analytics, and the new Manage SAP System Upgrade app.

The list of all new features including more information can be found on the What's New for SAP BTP ABAP environment page. The new AI features are listed separately on the What's New for ABAP Generative AI in ABAP Cloud. The first delivery of ADT for VS Code is independent from the ABAP Backend release and will be rolled-out separately. 

ABAP Language

  • Support of boolean expressions
  • Recursive references as enhancement of the ABAP Type System 
  • ABAP statement DELETE DUPLICATES without sorting

ABAP Development Tools (ADT)

  • Signature comparison during CDS Migration from DDIC-based views to CDS view entities (see Signature Comparison and Migrating CDS DDIC-based Views to CDS View Entities)
  • Using bracket highlighting for ABAP CDS objects
  • WYSIWYG editor support for Review Booklet pages with two data providers (details)

ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP)

  • Support of RAP Feature Control attributes readonly:create and mandatory:update (details)
  • Support of CDS Table Entities as draft persistence in RAP (details)

Analytics

  • Flexible Content Types for Review Booklets with Multiple Data Providers (details)
  • Enhanced App State Handling for Review Booklets (details)

Reuse Services

  • XCO Generation API for CDS Table Entities (details)
  • Maintain Translations app for the translation of email templates (details)

Business Configuration

  • You can now select Tree Table as table type in the SMBC object (details)

ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC)

  • Integration of ATC system in SAP BTP with ChaRM (details)
  • Improved ATC Check Results and Quick Fixes for SAP S/4HANA Custom Code Analysis 
  • New ATC Check Variant for Clean Core ABAP_CLEAN_CORE_READINESS to perform Clean Core analysis on SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA systems

Custom Code Analysis

  • Update accessible resources in Cloud Connector (see SAP Note 2861842 and SAP Note 3548772)
  • Manage Number of ATC Processes via Elastic Scaling (details)

SAP Fiori Launchpad

  • New app Manage News to author and manage news content that appears on the My Home page (details)
  • Reset Single Parameters in the Manage Launchpad Settings App (details)

Landscape Portal

  • Deploy Product: Install multiple add-ons in the same system (details)
  • New app Manage SAP System Upgrade (details)

Infrastructure

  • Transparent start time of the technical downtime for the next quartely upgrade (details)
  • Reduced minimum ABAP Runtime size with 0.5 ACU (details)
  • New Multi-Availability Zone Option with SAP HANA Cloud database configuration in a high-availability setup with a primary and a secondary instance in different availability zones (details)
  • Two new regions on Microsoft Azure: Toronto / Canada and Frankfurt / Germany (details)
